revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:今井野菊]] {{Infobox person}}
{{Multiple issues | citations missing = January 2024 | short footnotes = January 2024 }} '''Imai Nogiku''' (今井 野菊, {{ill|Imai Nogiku|ja|今井 野菊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Nogiku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, August 14, 1900 – 1982) was a Japanese local historian and poet. Her real name was '''Imai Sumie''' (今井 すみ江).<ref name="imaisuwa">Imai Nogiku, ''Suwa Monogatari'' (諏訪物語), Kokusho Kankōkai, 1975, p. 227.</ref>
== Early life and career == Born in {{ill|Miyagawa Village, Suwa District, Nagano Prefecture|ja|宮川村 (長野県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyagawa Village, Suwa District, Nagano Prefecture|1=Miyagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17176944}}, now part of {{ill|Chino, Nagano|en|Chino, Nagano|qid=Q838660|lt=Chino, Nagano|1=Chino, Nagano}}, Imai attended Suwa Girls’ High School (now {{ill|Nagano Prefecture Suwa Futaba High School|ja|長野県諏訪二葉高等学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagano Prefecture Suwa Futaba High School|1=Suwa Futaba High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11654381}}). While in her first year, she joined the literary magazine {{ill|Araragi (magazine)|en|Araragi (magazine)|qid=Q626208|lt=Araragi|1=Araragi (magazine)}}, studying under {{ill|Shimaki Akahiko|ja|島木赤彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimaki Akahiko|1=Akahiko Shimagi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11476347}} and {{ill|Moriyama Teisen|ja|森山汀川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moriyama Teisen|1=Teisen Moriyama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q60989880}}.<ref>Kitazawa Toshirō, "[https://www.city.chino.lg.jp/uploaded/attachment/14289.pdf Memorial Lecture: Akahiko and His Legacy]," Chino City, 2005, p. 11.</ref><ref name="chinocity">{{Cite web|title=Yatsugatake Literary Museum|url=https://www.city.chino.lg.jp/site/y-hakubutsukan/1643.html|website=City of Chino Official Website|access-date=October 5, 2024}}</ref>
In 1918, she graduated and became a substitute teacher at a local elementary school. The following year, she married Imai Heizaemon, a kanten (agar-agar) wholesaler.<ref name="imaisuwa"/><ref name="chinocity"/> A licensed instructor of the {{ill|Ikenobō|en|Ikenobō|qid=Q4022446|lt=Ikenobō School|1=Ikenobō}}, she was affectionately referred to as "the teacher of Chigamise" in her community.<ref name="suwagaku">Yamamoto Hiroko et al., ''Suwa Studies'' (諏訪学), Kokusho Kankōkai, 2018, pp. 418–420.</ref>
Following World War II, Imai dedicated herself to compiling the ''Old Miyagawa Village History'' while conducting extensive research on {{ill|Suwa Faith|ja|諏訪信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Faith|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪神社|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2370082}}, including the worship of {{ill|Mishaguji|en|Mishaguji|qid=Q11071209|lt=Mishaguji|1=Mishaguji}}, {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tenpaku faith|lt=Tenpaku faith|lt=Amabe no Kami|ja|天白神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amabe no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天白信仰}}. Her work included field surveys and preserving local heritage.<ref name="kobuzoku">Ancient Tribes Research Association, ''Ancient Suwa and the Mishaguji Ritual State,'' Ningen Publishing, 2017, p. 308.</ref> Imai passed away in 1982.<ref name="kobuzoku"/>
=== Contributions to Suwa Faith and Mishaguji Research === In 1953, alarmed by the destruction of ancient Kofuns and ruins near the {{ill|Maemiya Shrine of Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社 上社前宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maemiya Shrine of Suwa Taisha|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579219|1=Suwa Taisha Maemiya}}, Imai, together with historian Hosokawa Hayato, established the ''Old Miyagawa Village History Compilation Research Association.'' Initially attracting over 50 participants, the project gradually dwindled to just Imai. Immersed in the study of Mishaguji, Imai compiled her findings into 25 booklets distributed for free to local residents.
She also conducted field research across Japan, often traveling on foot and camping outdoors. Her seminal work, ''Mishaguji Field Survey Collection,'' was included in the publication ''Ancient Suwa and the Mishaguji Ritual State.'' In addition to Mishaguji, Imai explored other facets of Suwa Faith, such as {{ill|Chikato Worship|ja|チカト信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikato Worship|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135550810|1=Chikato Faith}} and {{ill|Tenpaku faith|lt=Tenpaku faith|lt=Amabe Worship|ja|天白信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amabe Worship|qq=}}.<ref name="suwagaku"/>
Her research influenced subsequent scholars, including {{ill|Yamada Sōmoku|ja|山田宗睦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada Sōmoku|1=Munemutsu Yamada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11470311}} and the Ancient Tribes Research Association. In 1974, the association members studied directly under Imai, further developing their findings in works such as ''Ancient Suwa and the Mishaguji Ritual State'' (1975) and ''The Origins and Development of Suwa Faith'' (1978).<ref name="suwagaku"/>
== Selected Poems == * "While rinsing rice, clear water flows / revealing white roots / and sprouting parsley" (1929)<ref name="tankashuu">Obi Sagyu (ed.), ''Suwa Tanka Collection,'' Nanshin Nichinichi Shinbunsha, 1929, p. 181.</ref> * "Kites circle the gentle waves of spring’s Sumida River, silent in flight" (1929)<ref name="tankashuu"/> * "We converse, brushing tobacco ash from palms / crouched in the narrow mountain path" (1929)<ref>''Araragi,'' Vol. 22, No. 12, 1929, p. 165.</ref> * "Wordlessly, I watch my son leave for war / tears fall as I prepare tea" (1944)<ref>''Araragi,'' Vol. 37, No. 5, 1944, p. 43.</ref>
== Major Works == * ''Suwa Tanka Collection'' (1929) * ''Clouds on the Horizon'' (1942, under the name Imai Sumie)<ref>''Nagano Prefectural Library General Catalog,'' 1977, p. 386.</ref> * ''The Tales of Suwa'' (1960) * ''Mishaguji Field Survey Collection'' (1975, included in ''Ancient Suwa and the Mishaguji Ritual State'')
== References == === Notes === {{Reflist}}
=== Further Reading === * {{Cite book|editor=Ancient Tribes Research Association|year=2017|title=Ancient Suwa and the Mishaguji Ritual State|publisher=Ningen Publishing|isbn=978-4-908-62715-6}} * {{Cite book|author=Yamamoto Hiroko et al.|year=2018|title=Suwa Studies|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|isbn=978-4-336-06254-3}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mishaguji|en|Mishaguji|qid=Q11071209|lt=Mishaguji|1=Mishaguji}} * {{ill|Tenpaku faith|lt=Tenpaku faith|lt=Amabe no Kami|ja|天白神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amabe no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天白信仰}} * {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}} * {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|qid=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}}
{{Suwa Faith}}
==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}
